---
title: "Networking Formula Sheet"
output: html_document
---

# Networking Formula Sheet

## 1. Transmission Delay

### Formula

Transmission Delay = Packet Size / Bandwidth

### Meaning

The time required to put all bits of a packet onto the transmission medium.

### Notes

- Larger packet size increases transmission delay
- Higher bandwidth decreases transmission delay

### Units

- Packet size: bits
- Bandwidth: bits/second
- Delay: seconds

---

## 2. Propagation Delay

### Formula

Propagation Delay = Distance / Propagation Speed

### Meaning

The time taken for a signal to travel from sender to receiver.

### Notes

- Depends on physical distance
- Depends on speed of signal in the medium

### Units

- Distance: meters
- Propagation speed: meters/second
- Delay: seconds

---

## 3. Bit Rate

### Formula

Bit Rate = Baud Rate × Bits per Symbol

### Meaning

Bit rate is the number of bits transmitted per second.

### Notes

- Baud rate = symbols per second
- Bits per symbol = number of bits represented by each symbol

### Units

- Bit rate: bits/second

---

## 4. Throughput

### Formula

Throughput = Successful Data Delivered / Time

### Meaning

The actual amount of useful data delivered per unit time.

### Notes

- Throughput is usually less than raw bandwidth
- It depends on congestion, protocol overhead, and errors

### Units

- bits/second or bytes/second

---

## 5. Efficiency

### Formula

Efficiency = Useful Output / Total Input

### Meaning

Measures how much of the transmitted data is actually useful.

### Notes

- Higher overhead reduces efficiency
- Used in networking performance analysis

### Units

- Percentage or ratio

---

## 6. Shannon Capacity Formula

### Formula

Channel Capacity = Bandwidth × log2(1 + SNR)

### Meaning

Gives the maximum theoretical data rate of a noisy channel.

### Where

- Bandwidth = channel bandwidth
- SNR = signal-to-noise ratio

### Notes

- More bandwidth increases capacity
- Higher SNR increases capacity

### Units

- bits/second

---

## 7. Nyquist Bit Rate Formula

### Formula

Maximum Data Rate = 2 × Bandwidth × log2(L)

### Meaning

Gives the maximum data rate of a noiseless channel.

### Where

- Bandwidth = channel bandwidth
- L = number of signal levels

### Notes

- Used for ideal noiseless channel
- More signal levels increase data rate

### Units

- bits/second

---

## 8. IPv4 Address Space

### Formula

Total IPv4 Addresses = 2^32

### Meaning

IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses, so total possible addresses are 2 raised to 32.

### Notes

- Actual usable addresses are fewer because of reserved ranges

---

## 9. IPv6 Address Space

### Formula

Total IPv6 Addresses = 2^128

### Meaning

IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses and supports a very large number of unique addresses.

---

## 10. CIDR Host Address Formula

### Formula

Number of host addresses = 2^(32 - prefix_length)

### Meaning

Used to calculate total addresses in an IPv4 subnet.

### Example

For /24:

2^(32 - 24) = 2^8 = 256

### Notes

- Usable host addresses are usually:

2^(32 - prefix_length) - 2

because network and broadcast addresses are reserved

---

## 11. Subnet Mask Relation

### Formula

Network bits + Host bits = 32

### Meaning

In IPv4, the 32 bits of the address are divided into network and host portions.

### Notes

- CIDR notation shows the network bits
- Example: /24 means 24 network bits and 8 host bits

---

## 12. Total Delay in Networks

### Formula

Total Delay = Transmission Delay + Propagation Delay + Queueing Delay + Processing Delay

### Meaning

The total time taken by a packet to travel from source to destination.

### Components

- Transmission delay: time to push bits onto link
- Propagation delay: time for signal to travel
- Queueing delay: waiting time in router or switch queue
- Processing delay: time to process packet header

---

## 13. Hamming Distance

### Formula

Hamming Distance = Number of differing bit positions

### Meaning

Measures how many bits differ between two binary strings of equal length.

### Notes

- Used in error detection and coding theory
- Smaller Hamming distance means more similarity

---

## 14. TCP Window Concept

### Formula

Window Size = Number of bytes that can be sent before acknowledgment

### Meaning

Used in TCP flow control to control how much data can be sent before ACK is received.

### Notes

- Larger window improves throughput
- Window size depends on receiver buffer and congestion

---

## 15. Goodput

### Formula

Goodput = Application Data Delivered / Time

### Meaning

Only the useful application data delivered, excluding protocol overhead and retransmissions.

### Notes

- Goodput is usually less than throughput

---

## 16. Utilization

### Formula

Utilization = Busy Time / Total Time

### Meaning

Shows how effectively a link or system is being used.

---

## 17. Packet Efficiency

### Formula

Packet Efficiency = Payload Size / Total Packet Size

### Meaning

Indicates how much of the packet is actual useful payload.

### Notes

- Higher header overhead means lower efficiency

---

## 18. Bandwidth-Delay Product

### Formula

Bandwidth-Delay Product = Bandwidth × Propagation Delay

### Meaning

Represents the amount of data that can be in transit in the network.

### Notes

- Very important in TCP window sizing
- Helps understand pipeline capacity

---

## 19. Error Rate

### Formula

Error Rate = Number of Errors / Total Number of Bits or Packets

### Meaning

Measures how often transmission errors occur.

---

## 20. Final Quick Revision Table

| Topic | Formula |
|---|---|
| Transmission Delay | Packet Size / Bandwidth |
| Propagation Delay | Distance / Propagation Speed |
| Bit Rate | Baud Rate × Bits per Symbol |
| Throughput | Successful Data Delivered / Time |
| Efficiency | Useful Output / Total Input |
| Shannon Capacity | Bandwidth × log2(1 + SNR) |
| Nyquist Bit Rate | 2 × Bandwidth × log2(L) |
| IPv4 Addresses | 2^32 |
| IPv6 Addresses | 2^128 |
| CIDR Host Count | 2^(32 - prefix_length) |
| Total Delay | TD + PD + QD + ProcD |
| Hamming Distance | Differing bit positions |
| Bandwidth-Delay Product | Bandwidth × Propagation Delay |

---

## 21. Exam Tips

- Memorize the formula names and their meanings.
- Know which formula applies to noiseless vs noisy channels.
- For subnetting, remember `2^(host bits)`.
- For delay questions, separate transmission and propagation delay.
- For TCP, focus on window size and bandwidth-delay product.
